data.title: Sustainable Fitch Affirms Minerva Foods’s Entity Rating at ‘3’
data.description:
Related Content: Minerva S.A. - Entity Rating

Sustainable Fitch-Sao Paulo-19 November 2025: Sustainable Fitch has affirmed Minerva S.A.’s (Minerva Foods) Entity Rating at ‘3’ and increased its entity score to 52 from 49. This reflects the company’s robust sustainability reporting and commitments and its progress in monitoring its suppliers.

Minerva Foods is a Brazilian company that operates in the slaughtering and meat processing industry. Minerva Foods is South America's leading beef exporter and Brazil's third-largest beef slaughterhouse.

Livestock farming accounted for 17% of global GHG emissions, with livestock emissions (enteric fermentation and waste management) being the most representative. In Brazil, there is growing attention on the sector to monitor its supply chain and eliminate identified illegal practices, including irregular deforestation, and to reduce GHG emissions.

The company’s environmental profile is affected by the negative environmental impact of its slaughter and meat processing activities and the environmental risk associated with its supplier chain. The company has good environmental management practices, with robust commitments and policies, but its environmental indicators have worsened due to the acquisition of 13 new industrial units in 2024. This indicates a need to intensify actions to meet short- and long-term objectives.

We consider Minerva Foods’s activities to have a neutral social impact and rate its social responsibility, labour and diversity practices as average.

Minerva Foods’s governance profile is also average. The company’s good governance and risk management structure is limited by the low independence of its board and the high pay disparity between the highest-paid executive and the median employee.

GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT SUSTAINABLE FITCH RATINGS AND OTHER PRODUCTS

Sustainable Fitch's Ratings offer a global solution to assess Environmental, Social and Governance performance of all asset classes at an entity, framework and instrument level, helping the financial community make better, informed decisions. It has developed a modular analysis framework, enabling it to provide a Second-Party Opinion (SPO) and a monitored suite of Sustainable Fitch Ratings including Entity Rating, Framework Rating and Instrument Rating.

Sustainable Fitch Ratings indicate an entity’s performance, commitment, and integration of environmental and social considerations into its business, strategy and management, and the effectiveness of governance. The ratings are provided on a scale of ‘1’ to ‘5’, with ‘1’ being best and ‘5’ the worst, and scores of ‘0’ to ‘100’ where ‘0’ is the worst and ‘100’ is the best.

data.title: Boeing Wins $13 Billion Flydubai Deal For 737 Max
data.description:
Boeing just secured a major lift, signing a new agreement with Flydubai for up to 150 of its 737 Max jets. Flydubai says the deal is worth about $13 billion, and it comes only days after the airline placed its first-ever order with Airbus a clear sign the carrier is gearing up for a big expansion and is happy to work with both manufacturers.

The memorandum of understanding includes 75 firm 737 Max orders and options for another 75 if Flydubai decides to widen its fleet even further. The airline already operates 96 Boeing 737s, so sticking with the Max fits neatly into how it runs its operations today. Once finalized, this will add to Flydubai's already large pipeline of Max aircraft still waiting to be delivered.

For Boeing, the timing couldn't be better. With production stability and customer confidence still top of mind, landing a headline order like this gives the planemaker some much-needed momentum. this is a big fleet bet at a moment when global aircraft supply, demand and delivery timelines are all under close watch.

data.title: Lambda Raises $1.5 Billion To Power Massive AI Buildout
data.description:
Lambda has raised more than $1.5 billion in new Series E funding, giving the company a huge lift as it races to build the AI infrastructure everyone suddenly needs. CEO Stephen Balaban said the cash will go straight into creating gigawatt-scale AI factories massive compute hubs designed to power the apps and services that hundreds of millions of people use every day. He put the company's goal in simple terms: making AI compute as easy to access as electricity, one person, one GPU.

The round was led by TWG Global, the investment firm backed by Thomas Tull and Mark Walter, with several longtime Lambda investors jumping back in. Lambda describes itself as a superintelligence cloud company, offering the high-performance GPU clusters that developers and enterprises increasingly rely on. Nvidia isn't just a supplier it's also an investor and a customer.

Lambda is reportedly eyeing a 2026 IPO and wants to establish itself as one of the core infrastructure players behind the AI boom. as AI demand explodes, compute is becoming the new gold rush.

data.title: Abbott is weighing takeover of Exact Sciences, Bloomberg News reports
data.description: Medical device maker Abbott Laboratories is nearing a potential takeover of cancer test maker Exact Sciences Corp , Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

data.title: Novartis to build manufacturing hub in North Carolina, creating 700 jobs
data.description:
Novartis will expand its operations in North Carolina and build a manufacturing hub there as part of a planned $23 billion of U.S. infrastructure investment over the next five years, the Swiss pharmaceuticals company said on Wednesday.

The expansion is projected to create 700 new positions at Novartis and more than 3,000 indirect jobs across the supply chain by 2030, the company's statement said.

The announcement follows a preliminary deal struck by the U.S. and Swiss governments last week to cut U.S. tariffs on Switzerland to 15% from 39%.

Central to the deal is a pledge by Swiss companies such as Novartis to invest $200 billion in the U.S. by the end of 2028.

Novartis said the new hub, expected to open in 2027 or 2028, will comprise two new facilities in Durham, North Carolina, for biologics manufacturing and sterile packaging, and a site in Morrisville for solid dosage production and packaging.

Novartis said it will also expand its existing Durham campus to support sterile filling of biologics.

The expansion is designed to increase the company's manufacturing capacity so that all of its key U.S. medicines can be produced domestically, it said.

data.title: Brazil's Lula makes diplomatic push for early climate deal at COP30 summit
data.description:
Key points:
  • Brazil's Lula set to meet key negotiators
  • COP30 host seeking early deal on contentious issues
  • Fresh negotiating texts still not released

By Kate Abnett and Lisandra Paraguassu

Brazil's president was meeting with key negotiators at the COP30 summit on Wednesday as part of a drive to land an early deal on some of the most divisive issues in the global climate talks, including fossil fuels and climate finance.

The two-week U.N. summit in the Amazon city of Belem has brought nearly 200 countries together to try to ratchet up multilateral action to limit climate change, despite the absence of the U.S., the top historic greenhouse gas emitter.

But rifts on key issues remain, posing a fresh test of the international will to slow global warming.

Host Brazil, hoping to buck the trend in which recent climate summits ran well past deadline, seeks to endorse a package of agreements later on Wednesday, and the outstanding issues on Friday. But it is already facing delays publishing new negotiating texts.

FRESH DRAFT EXPECTED ON WEDNESDAY

The COP30 presidency had planned to land a fresh draft of the initial deal early on Wednesday, but no announcements had been issued by early afternoon. Negotiators told Reuters tough talks were ongoing.

The first version of the deal published on Tuesday had presented a range of options that split opinion.

Brazil and around 80 other supportive nations want to agree something that helps spur action on a 2023 agreement made at COP28 to transition away from fossil fuels, the main source of greenhouse gas emissions.

However, the idea of creating a roadmap to help guide that transition had so far been rejected by others, Brazil's COP30 President Andre Correa do Lago said on Tuesday.

President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva arrived back at the conference on Wednesday, giving renewed political impetus to the talks. He was expected to meet key negotiators as well as U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres.

'WE'VE GOT BLOCKERS,' VANUATU SAYS

Pacific island nation Vanuatu's climate minister Ralph Regenvanu told Reuters Saudi Arabia was one of those opposed to the fossil fuel plan.

Saudi Arabia did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

"I think it's going to be very difficult ... because we've got blockers," Regenvanu said.

Other island nations said the issue was vital.

"We're going to have to fight tooth and nail. There are many parties who have already said that they do not want that in the text at all," Marshall Islands climate envoy Tina Stege told Reuters.

A coalition of 100 organisations, including companies like Volvo and Unilever, sent a letter to the COP30 presidency expressing support for a roadmap to transition away from fossil fuel use, saying it would help countries and businesses plan the shift to cleaner energy.

CLIMATE FINANCE

Other contentious issues in the package include pinning down how rich countries will provide finance to poorer countries to switch to clean energy, and what must be done about a gap between promised emissions cuts and those needed to stop temperatures rising.

Poorer countries already bearing the impacts of global warming are rallying for a strong outcome.

"We want ambition on finance. We want ambition on adaptation. We want to see ambition on the transition," Jiwoh Abdulai, Sierra Leone's climate minister, told Reuters. "And we want to ensure that we live here on a path that is sustainable, not just for this generation, but for future generations."

Plans to launch a U.N.-backed global market for trading carbon offset credits have hit a snag as governments dispute over the funding to get the market up and running, five sources told Reuters.

data.title: S&P 500 Rallies on Alphabet Jump. Nvidia Earnings Spark Market Optimism
data.description:
Broad market moves were muted on Wednesday as investors rotated into AI names ahead of Nvidia earnings.

The S&P 500 rose 0.2%, the Nasdaq Composite climbed 0.5% and the Dow slipped 0.3%, according to market data.

Alphabet led gains, jumping about 5% after rolling out its Gemini 3 model, lifting tech sentiment.

Alphabet's jump offset earlier losses across chip suppliers, which have seen heavy selling amid concern over AI capex sustainability this week.

Nvidia gained more than 2% in early trade as traders prepared for results after the close. Analysts expect strong data-center revenue, but the company faces a high bar on guidance.

Market watchers say Nvidia's outlook on hyperscaler spending will set near-term direction for AI-exposed stocks. Some strategists warn valuation pressure remains and cautious guidance could spark more selling in chips.

Retailers and other cyclicals showed mixed reactions to earnings, keeping the market tone fragile.

Investors will focus on Nvidia's report and management comments after the bell for clues on enterprise AI spending and year-end momentum.

data.title: Target Slips As Outlook Tightens And Sales Stay Weak
data.description:
Target slipped more than 2% in pre-market trading Wednesday after the retailer cut its full-year profit outlook and logged another quarter of sluggish demand. The company now expects FY25 earnings of $7$8 per share, narrowing the top end of its previous $7$9 range. Q3 earnings were a small bright spot, with adjusted EPS of $1.78 beating expectations of $1.71.

Revenue came in at $25.3 billion, matching estimates but down 1.5% from last year. Comparable sales fell 2.7%, a deeper drop than analysts anticipated, as shoppers continued to pull back on non-essential categories like apparel and home decor. Incoming CEO Michael Fiddelke, who steps in next February, said the quarter was volatile, pointing to pressure from the SNAP funding pause and the government shutdown.

Target expects similar softness in Q4 and is guiding for a low single-digit sales decline. A fuller outlook will arrive after the holidays. The company also plans to add another $1 billion to next year's investment budget, bringing total new spending to $5 billion aimed at improving stores, merchandise and digital systems.

data.title: US canceling October's employment report after shutdown prevented data collection
data.description:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said on Wednesday it would not be publishing the closely watched employment report for October, but will combine nonfarm payrolls for that month with November's report after the recently ended government shutdown prevented the collection of data for the household survey.

That means October's unemployment rate will never be known, something that the White House had warned would happen.

"Establishment survey data from the Current Employment Statistics survey for October 2025 will be published with the November 2025 data," BLS said in a statement. "Household survey data from the Current Population Survey could not be collected for the October 2025 reference period due to a lapse in appropriations. The household survey data is not able to be retroactively collected."

The establishment survey covers the nonfarm payrolls component of the employment report. The unemployment rate and other ratios are calculated from the household survey.

The BLS will on Thursday publish September's employment report, which was delayed by the 43-day government shutdown.

November's employment report will be released on December 16, the BLS said. That would be after the Federal Reserve's December 9-10 policy meeting.

"The collection period for November 2025 data will be extended for both surveys, and extra processing time will be added," the agency said.

data.title: Cash App to Launch Buy Now, Pay Later Debit Card
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Block's payment platform Cash App is launching a new debit card that will allow holders to pay in installments at any location where Visa is accepted.

Cash App said Wednesday it plans to debut the Visa Debit Flex Card, which will give users the option to pay with Block's buy now, pay later feature Afterpay. Using Afterpay, customers can take out a loan to buy something and pay it off incrementally over the course of six weeks.

Afterpay purchases can be made anywhere online or in-store where Visa is accepted, the company said. Customers can order a Visa Debit Flex Card and turn Afterpay on and off through the Cash App app.

Cash App's news comes as buy now, pay later companies such as Affirm and Klarna are pushing their debit card offerings. Offering a card is designed to make it easier for customers to use buy now, pay later services with more vendors and in more situations. The loans have become more popular among consumers in recent years as many of them come with 0% interest, unlike traditional credit-card debt.

Cash App will start the program as a pilot in the coming months, with plans for a larger rollout in early 2026, it said.

Instead of looking backward at credit scores, Cash App's platform determines eligibility for a buy now, pay later loan by evaluating customers' current financial behaviors, such as cash-flow patterns, spending habits, savings and payment consistency.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com
